source_title: #37 Annie Duke: Getting Better by Being Wrong

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: Welcome to the Knowledge Project. My guest today is former professional poker player Annie Duke. Her book Thinking in Bets is about how we can make smarter decisions when we don't have all the facts. In this deep dive conversation, she offers insight into poker, the cognitive biases that lead us astray, and a master class in decision-making.

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: I was studying syntactic bootstrapping, which is the theory that children learn their first language because they have an innate grammar built in. Right at the end of it, I got sick and landed in the hospital for two weeks. I missed that season of the academic job market, so I went to Montana to recuperate. I started playing poker mainly out of a need for money. The only reason I ended up getting into it was because my brother had gotten really into chess and poker. He lost his college fund—sixty-three hundred dollars—in that first year, but righted the ship pretty quickly.

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: What were those early days of poker like for you? Something few people would have the courage to pursue, especially in that time context.

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: People would say, what do you do for a living? I would say I'm a poker player. They would ask what my husband did because they were curious what I actually did for money to support this horrible evil gambling habit. When I explained I was actually the main financial support of the family, they really wouldn't believe it. Very often it would devolve into, have you thought about Gamblers Anonymous? The game itself was amazing—this really incredible exercise in constantly revealing that you know less than you thought you did five minutes ago. As you get better at the game, you realize you know less and less about the game.

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: How did your study of psychology influence your poker playing?

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: I realized all of the work I'd done in really understanding how people make decisions, cognitive bias, how people construct mental models of the world—how people learn in really uncertain systems—was informing the way I thought about poker. I approached it as a decision-making problem. Here's the puzzle I had immediately: I was playing with people forty years older than me who had been playing that much longer and had really clearly stalled in their learning about thirty-nine years before. They were getting feedback—they weren't winning—and yet they weren't learning.

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: I had a conversation with Eric Seidel very early on. I went up to him after some tough loss saying I was playing so well and that guy got really lucky. His response was very different. He said something like, why are you telling me this story? Do you think I really want to hear this? I've lost with a lot of hands too. If you really lost because of bad luck, what's the point of the story? There's nothing you can learn from it. Now if you have a question about strategy, we'll sit down and talk. But don't just come up and tell me about bad luck. You're literally wasting my time and it's annoying.

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: That must have still been hard though. How do you go from your default of explaining things away to being part of a group and constantly being told, here's what you don't see about you?

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: When Eric said that to me I was really sad. I almost cried. I wanted him to approve of me, to think I was worth talking to. But the social group can act as the best piece of cheese ever. Getting you to think I'm worthwhile to talk to feels really good. So Eric, my brother, these people who I really wanted to think well of me weren't going to tolerate this type of talk. I needed to go to them with questions. Now I would say, I won this hand but I think I really butchered it. Their face would light up—total engagement. The gratification is from the group. They're giving you approval for that kind of thinking.

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: When you know you are being examined—whether on television or in an organization—resulting really matters. When I'm on TV thinking about a decision that may have a bad outcome, what's in my head is not is this decision good or bad, it's are people gonna think the decision is good or bad because I happen to have lost the hand. If I know the person in the leadership role is gonna look at outcomes and critique me when things turn out poorly, it's gonna make me make safer decisions—more bulletproof to criticism, more normal. It's an innovation killer.

paragraphs.speaker: annie
paragraphs.name: Annie Duke
paragraphs.text: There's twenty-six seconds left in the Super Bowl, 2015.
